mHwonDEAAAAJ.keywords: Computer Networking, Network Algorithmics, Data Streaming Algorithms, Performance Evaluation, Bloom Filters, Sketching Algorithms, Per-flow Traffic Measurement, Network Traffic Entropy Estimation, IP Traceback and DDoS Mitigation, IP Address Anonymization
mHwonDEAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Jun Xu is a Professor of Computer Science at the Georgia Institute of Technology, where he leads a research group within the College of Computing focused on the intersection of **computer networking** and **algorithmic foundations**. His work spans both theory and systems: he develops *efficient streaming algorithms*, designs practical network measurement and monitoring systems, and evaluates performance at scale. Central to his research are <u>per-flow traffic measurement</u>, <u>compact data structures for high-speed packet processing</u>, and <u>robust defenses and tracing techniques for network security</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Jun Xu’s research bridges **network algorithmics**, **data streaming algorithms**, and applied **performance evaluation** for real-world networks. He has pioneered compact sketching and bloom-filter variants for high-speed measurement (e.g., the Space-code Bloom Filter), developed streaming techniques for estimating flow size distributions and entropy of traffic to enable anomaly detection and traffic classification, and proposed packet-logging and traceback schemes to mitigate and localize DDoS attacks. His work also covers traffic matrix inference, flow- and counter-architecture designs (BRICK and related counter arrays), prefix-preserving anonymization of traffic traces, and scalable query/routing strategies for peer-to-peer networks. More recently, he has applied machine learning and syslog analysis to datacenter reliability (failure diagnosis and prediction), and explored algorithmic problems in representative databases, skylines, and approximate nearest neighbor search — demonstrating a consistent pattern of translating algorithmic insight into deployable network measurement and management tools.

---

## Notable Works
- "Space-code bloom filter for efficient traffic flow measurement" (2003) — Introduced the Space-code Bloom Filter, a memory- and computation-efficient sketch for accurate per-flow measurement at wire speeds, enabling finer-grained accounting and anomaly detection in high-speed networks.
- "Data streaming algorithms for efficient and accurate estimation of flow size distribution" (2004) — Developed streaming estimators and sampling strategies that substantially improve accuracy of flow-size distribution inference from limited observations, with applications to traffic engineering and workload characterization.
- "IP traceback-based intelligent packet filtering: A novel technique for defending against Internet DDoS attacks" (2003) — Proposed an active traceback-driven filtering approach that mitigates ongoing DDoS attacks by combining traceback information with selective packet filtering, contributing both practical defense mechanisms and theoretical foundations for large-scale traceback.

---

## Academic Background
Jun Xu is a long-standing faculty member of the Georgia Tech College of Computing and maintains an active research homepage (http://www.cc.gatech.edu/~jx). Over a multi-decade career he has produced a sustained and highly-cited body of work (total citations: 7094) spanning top-tier conferences and journals in networking, algorithms, and systems. His publication record shows a consistent pattern of collaborative, interdisciplinary research connecting theoretical algorithm design with practical network measurement, security, and performance evaluation. He has supervised graduate students and collaborated with industry and academic partners on measurement-driven projects, and his contributions have been widely adopted and cited across the networking research community.

## Overview
Francisco Romero is a researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, working at the intersection of **computer systems**, **computer architecture**, *databases*, and **AI**. His work is situated within Georgia Tech’s systems and architecture communities and is centered on designing scalable, cost‑efficient platforms for ML-driven applications. Romero’s research emphasizes <u>automated, model‑agnostic inference serving</u>, <u>serverless and transient execution</u> for large‑scale workloads, and <u>performance‑aware video analytics</u>, combining systems‑level engineering with algorithmic and scheduling innovations to make ML and data systems easier to deploy and operate.

---

## Research Areas
Romero’s research spans practical and theoretical problems in modern infrastructure for AI and data processing. He has led work on inference serving that removes the need for exhaustive model‑variant tuning—pioneering approaches to *model‑less* selection and management (see “{INFaaS}: Automated model-less inference serving” and “A case for managed and model-less inference serving”). He explores serverless and transient execution paradigms to outsource everyday jobs to massively parallel, ephemeral containers and functions (e.g., “From laptop to lambda: Outsourcing everyday jobs to thousands of transient functional containers” and “Faa $ t: A transparent auto-scaling cache for serverless applications”), addressing latency, cost, and state management challenges. In heterogeneous and multi‑tenant environments he develops interference‑aware and online scheduling techniques to improve utilization and meet latency SLOs (e.g., “Mage: Online and interference-aware scheduling for multi-scale heterogeneous systems” and “Interference-aware scheduling for inference serving”). Another major thread is video analytics systems design—declarative model relationships, pipeline auto‑tuning, and vision‑language integration—expressed in projects such as “Llama: A heterogeneous & serverless framework for auto-tuning video analytics pipelines”, “Optimizing video analytics with declarative model relationships”, “VIVA: An End-to-End System for Interactive Video Analytics”, and “Zelda: Video analytics using vision-language models”. More recently, Romero has extended these systems perspectives to latency‑critical model selection and novel interfaces for IoT/LLM fusion (e.g., “Model selection for latency-critical inference serving” and “Flash-Fusion: Enabling Expressive, Low-Latency Queries on IoT Sensor Streams with LLMs”).

## Notable Works
- {INFaaS}: Automated model-less inference serving (2021, 453 citations) — Introduced a model‑agnostic inference serving design that automates selection among model variants to optimize cost and latency without requiring manual per‑model tuning, significantly improving ease‑of‑use and operational efficiency for large‑scale inference deployments.  
- From laptop to lambda: Outsourcing everyday jobs to thousands of transient functional containers (2019, 306 citations) — Demonstrated gg, a framework and CLI tools that harness cloud function services to parallelize common developer tasks across thousands of transient containers, achieving near‑interactive completion times for traditionally long‑running jobs.  
- Faa $ t: A transparent auto-scaling cache for serverless applications (2021, 179 citations) — Proposed a transparent caching layer tailored for FaaS platforms that enables stateful, low‑latency serverless applications by auto‑scaling cache capacity and hiding state management complexity from developers.

## Academic Background
Romero’s publication record, stretching from interdisciplinary applications in psycholinguistics and behavioral coding (2015) to cutting‑edge systems and AI platforms (2018–2025), reflects a trajectory through both applied ML and systems research. He is affiliated with the **Georgia Institute of Technology** and collaborates broadly across computer architecture, databases, and AI research communities. His work has accrued over *1,400 citations* (total citations: **1486**), indicating strong community impact across conference and journal venues. Romero’s research has been recognized via high‑impact publications and influential system releases that have shaped thinking about inference serving, serverless state management, heterogeneous scheduling, and video analytics; he maintains an active homepage documenting projects and software (https://www.franciscoaromero.com/) and continues to engage with industry and academic partners to transfer system innovations into production environments.
